channel.title: The Economist - World in Brief
channel.description: Catch up quickly on the global stories that matter

item.title: World in Brief - March 13, 2026
item.description:
America temporarily eased sanctions on Russian oil and petroleum currently at sea. The authorisation, part of efforts to stabilise global energy markets, will last until April 11th. Britain announced it would not follow suit. Asian stockmarkets slid on Friday after Brent crude, the global oil benchmark, jumped back above $100 a barrel.

Israel launched a fresh wave of attacks on central Tehran and against Hizbullah targets in Lebanon. Earlier an American refuelling plane crashed in Iraq. The Pentagon said that neither “hostile fire or friendly fire” was to blame. Four crew members were killed and two more were missing. A French soldier was killed by a drone attack on a military base in the Erbil region of Iraq.

Britain’s economy did not grow in January, according to the Office for National Statistics. Analysts expected a 0.2% increase. Growth was 0.1% in December. The latest figures showed the services sector was flat, while production fell by 0.1%. This week Sir Keir Starmer, Britain’s prime minister, warned that a longer war in the Middle East would harm the country’s economy.

Mark Carney, Canada’s prime minister, announced plans to invest an additional $25.7bn in defence and infrastructure projects in the Arctic. “We’re taking full responsibility for defending our sovereignty,” he said, ahead of a meeting with his German and Norwegian counterparts in Oslo on Friday. Canada has refocused attention on the Arctic following threats from Russia and Mr Trump’s expansionist rhetoric.

Michelle Bowman, vice-chair of the Federal Reserve, announced that America’s central bank will soon cut capital requirements for major banks. The move is part of a wider easing of restrictions that were introduced following the 2007-09 financial crisis. Wall Street banks have claimed for years that an overhaul is needed in order to boost economic growth.

A gunman who rammed a car into a synagogue in Michigan was found dead inside his vehicle. According to officials, the suspect, a naturalised Lebanese man, drove the car “with purpose” down a hallway inside the building, which houses a school. Police reportedly found explosives inside the vehicle. One security guard was injured. The synagogue confirmed that all students and staff were safe.

Researchers at Cambridge University called for stricter regulation of AI toys. Their study of how children aged three to five interacted with a cuddly toy, Gabbo, is one of the world’s first into the effects of such technology on preschoolers. They found that children were often interrupted, talked over and left confused by the toy, sparking concerns about the impact on their social development.

An extraordinary Quds Day in Iran

In Iran the last Friday of Ramadan is marked by state-backed demonstrations against Israel. Ahead of this year’s Quds Day (“al-Quds” is Arabic for “Jerusalem”) the regime’s new leader urged Iranians to turn out for rallies regardless of continuing strikes. They have, despite Israel announcing its intention to bomb Tehran on Friday morning. Clips of a large explosion at a square packed with demonstrators—some shouting “Death to Israel” or America—are being circulated on Iranian media.

It is tradition for Iran’s leader to publicly express support for Palestine, too. But Mojtaba Khamenei has not yet appeared. His call for Iranians to take to the streets came from a written address read by a presenter on state television. It was the first heard from him since his ascension on Monday. Rumours abound about whether Mr Khamenei was injured in the same attack that killed his father and predecessor. The suffering of Palestinians in Gaza, meanwhile, has been compounded since the start of the conflict, which has crippled aid efforts.

Trump’s popularity may be on a slippery slope

“When oil prices go up, we make a lot of money,” boasted Donald Trump this week, dismissing fears about his war’s financial fallout by noting that America is the world’s largest oil producer. By “we”, he must mean American oil companies—the country is also among the world’s largest oil exporters. For the public, the average gallon of petrol now costs over $3.50 for the first time since 2024, when Mr Trump campaigned on the Democrats’ failure to constrain prices.

That should worry him. The president has not received the patriotic bump in popularity common at the beginning of military campaigns. But his base still has his back: in a recent poll 83% of Republicans approved of his handling of Iran, and self-described “MAGA” Republicans are even more supportive. But voters hate high fuel prices, and Republicans are likelier to rely on gas-guzzling cars than more-urban Democrats. “The longer this goes, the more risk there is to the president,” says Don Bacon, a Republican congressman.

Sign up to enjoy a mind-expanding mix of stories, delivered six days a week

Carney and Merz share an Arctic rendezvous

Across the snowy marshes of northern Norway and Finland, some 32,500 soldiers from 14 NATO countries are currently training for Arctic warfare. The ten-day routine exercise, known as Cold Response, is usually intended as a signal to Russia alone. This year, it has a wider audience.

Donald Trump has made brazen attempts to annex Greenland, arguing that NATO allies are not doing enough to protect the Arctic. On Friday Mark Carney, Canada’s prime minister, and Friedrich Merz, Germany’s chancellor, will join their Norwegian counterpart on the front lines to send a clear message to America: NATO allies are stepping up.

The transatlantic alliance’s power structure is shifting, too. NATO is bolstering its northern presence through the Arctic Sentry, an initiative to monitor the region’s airspace and waters. Meanwhile, command of the alliance’s Atlantic and Arctic headquarters is set to transfer from an American to a Briton. But now Mr Trump is tangled up in a war in the Middle East, NATO’s show of strength may fail to get his attention.

Rupert Murdoch’s family saga on screen

“Dynasty: The Murdochs” acknowledges that it is not the first show inspired by the family’s story. “Succession”, a wildly popular television drama about four siblings jostling for power over their elderly father’s media conglomerate, was first conceived as a mock-documentary about Rupert Murdoch’s empire. But this is the real thing: a vivid and informed four-part Netflix series that switches between the rise of the Murdoch dynasty and the recent machinations of Mr Murdoch’s offspring to control it after his death—an eventuality he declines to acknowledge.

The result is a study in how life imitates art. “Succession”, which sparked panic in the junior Murdoch camps, fuelled the boardroom battles that “The Murdochs” details. And while their story is entertaining, in both fact and fiction, its consequences are material. As one contributor notes, “It’s a family squabble that has a huge effect on our politics and on our lives.”

Daily quiz

We will serve you a new question each weekday. On Friday your challenge is to give us all five answers and tell us the theme. Email your responses (and your home city and country) by 1700 GMT on Friday to QuizEspresso@economist.com. We’ll pick three winners at random and crown them on Saturday.

Friday: Which word links the venues for a circus performance and a boxing match?

Thursday: What is another term for someone who works as a stable hand?

Don’t play for safety. It’s the most dangerous thing in the world.

Hugh Walpole
